nodes.label: Bloomerang
nodes.before_pain_summary: Attribution broken across messy Salesforce architecture, manual Tableau reporting, and failing Marketo Measure implementation; no predictability into where marketing spend yields pipeline and revenue; nonprofit buyer data behaves like B2C with Gmail addresses and duplicates
nodes.after_with_revsure: Full-funnel AI attribution with predictive pipeline projections, modular deployment, no per-integration fees, and embedded customer success team that helps build and maintain custom dashboards and data models
after_wins_realized: Pipeline projections module configured
Daily pacing sheet replicated in platform
Metric renaming capability enabled
Site script page tracking working
Form tracking fixed by moving to footer
Demand Gen V2 dashboard with visual summaries
UTM content filtering for message comparison
Mass filter application across widgets
MQA first/last touch channel pacing
after_open_issues: Need to validate custom object support for MQA model
Monthly forecasting model needs configuration
Data integrity initiatives may require coordination with RevSure team
Need to convince RevOps team to move off active Marketo Measure implementation
SSO/Okta IDP flow still being debugged
Google Ads campaign integration pending
Channel classification rules incomplete (many campaigns in 'other')
Email send attribution rules need clarification
Data validation for spend data pending
MQA internal pass exclusion needs implementation
Daily pacing tracker actuals showing zero
MQA discrepancy resolution
Brand dashboard overview page
Volume and value simultaneous display
Landing page campaign touch conversion

nodes.text: we're in a very interesting position at Gainsight where we're the category creator. And I would argue 95% of the CS market already exists in our database. So you have new lead in contact creation as folks are changing companies and stuff like that. But typically what we see is like there is no new lead created and then converted to opportunity within a couple months. That just doesn't happen. Typically, they've interfaced with Gainsight in some way like years ago.

But we found it to be particularly helpful getting s SAS to convert over into SQL and pipeline. So don't use it as a top of funnel thing. Use it as something to reengage the S ones and that's how you get the most bang for your buck.

nodes.label: LinkedIn has strong impact on bookings but weak impact on pipeline generation - a counterintuitive finding
nodes.claim: LinkedIn has strong impact on bookings but weak impact on pipeline generation - a counterintuitive finding
nodes.why_unusual: Typically LinkedIn is measured on top-of-funnel pipeline; market mix modeling showed 57% booking impact but minimal pipeline impact, and specifically helps low-value accounts not high-value ones

nodes.text: What we're showing for pipeline generation volume, this blue is the impact of LinkedIn, which, which is not that great, but, you know, that's its average influence. Let me roof this down so you can see. Yeah. But when we look at booking volume, it's a 57% impact. So it is significantly impacting booking.
nodes.speaker: Harry Hawk

nodes.label: LinkedIn leads convert better but sales works them less, suggesting sales behavior not lead quality is the problem
nodes.claim: LinkedIn leads convert better but sales works them less, suggesting sales behavior not lead quality is the problem
nodes.why_unusual: Typically marketing gets blamed for poor lead quality; here data shows sales prioritization is the issue despite better marketing performance

nodes.text: this is the classic issue that marketing faces is that you know, when sales does get a hot lead and they're working it, they tend to work their own leads harder

nodes.claim: Blog posts may be 'moment in time' content that should be scaled back versus evergreen product pages

nodes.text: maybe we could find a way to scale back the blogs because they're kind of like a moment in time thing. We see it in the page visits, we see it in like how long people are reading them, but then we also see them in, you know, how they're moving people along in sales cycle

nodes.label: Spending 10x more on Google than LinkedIn but getting same long-term user contribution
nodes.claim: Spending 10x more on Google than LinkedIn but getting same long-term user contribution
nodes.why_unusual: Contradicts typical last-touch attribution that would heavily favor Google; shows value of multi-touch modeling for brand channels

nodes.text: even though there was this 10 to one dispar even know difference in spend, they both almost contributed the same amount towards long-term users, which in this case was like 20% versus seven 17 or 18%.
nodes.speaker: Harry Hawk

nodes.label: MMX is a counterfactual to attribution, not a replacement — you need both for a 360-degree view, and where they disagree is equally important as where they agree
nodes.claim: MMX is a counterfactual to attribution, not a replacement — you need both for a 360-degree view, and where they disagree is equally important as where they agree
nodes.why_unusual: Most vendors position their solution as the single source of truth; RevSure explicitly says you need four different models (lift test, FCA, MMX, DGE) and should arbitrage between them

nodes.text: when you combine lift test fca and mmx along with whatever you're doing in dge you've got kind of a 360 degree view you're going to have different opinions that you're going to have to arbitrage and take position on but you're going to have four really good um positions and then obviously where they do agree that's interesting and but i think it's equally important where they disagree right
nodes.speaker: Harry Hawk

nodes.label: Traditional attribution's cause-and-effect logic is fundamentally limited compared to multivariable impact modeling
nodes.claim: Traditional attribution's cause-and-effect logic is fundamentally limited compared to multivariable impact modeling
nodes.why_unusual: While many vendors critique last-touch, explicitly calling out that even multi-touch attribution misses cross-channel incremental effects is less common; the BirdEye example of Facebook driving 5% compounded branded search growth for 10-15 weeks after being turned off illustrates this dramatically

nodes.text: in traditional attribution, you know, it's very cause and effect, right? Someone clicked on an ad, they filled out a form. and we're attributing, you know, okay, well, they clicked on the ad, so we're giving credit to that ad, right? Or multi-touch, we consider several touch points, where MMX is looking at not only the statistics of how all of these impact, but it's also looking at the multivariable impact, meaning we know at BirdEye, we did a test They had Facebook off, they turned it on, spent a quarter million dollars in 15 days. And then for the next 10 to 15 weeks, 5% compounded week over week growth in branded search.
nodes.speaker: Harry Hawk

nodes.label: MMX predictions are fixed at quarter start and NOT updated — deliberately not real-time
nodes.claim: MMX predictions are fixed at quarter start and NOT updated — deliberately not real-time
nodes.why_unusual: Most SaaS analytics dashboards emphasize real-time or near-real-time updates; explaining that the model is intentionally static until next quarter is counterintuitive and requires client education

nodes.text: the part that to me that's important to understand because we thought we when i first saw that we thought well let's come back next week and see how it's changed but it's not updated until the beginning of the quarter so it's based on all of last quarter's results
nodes.speaker: Harry Hawk

nodes.why_unusual: Most marketing ops teams default to Marketo as source of truth for email engagement; deliberately choosing Salesforce campaign member statuses over native Marketo integration is contrarian

nodes.label: They don't use forecast category in Salesforce and forecasting is done top-down rather than bottom-up
nodes.claim: They don't use forecast category in Salesforce and forecasting is done top-down rather than bottom-up
nodes.why_unusual: Most mature SaaS companies use Salesforce forecast categories and bottom-up forecasting; Lyra's manual top-down approach with rep-adjusted 'at risk'/'commit' fields is less common

nodes.text: So we do have something called manual forecast category where the rep is basically saying, for example, like if they feel like they're not gonna win it, they do at risk, right? If they do feel like they're gonna win it, they would do something like commit. And that's a manual thing that the rep is changing.

nodes.label: They don't want to use out-of-box probability percentage; instead use projected win rate as just a projection/expectation
nodes.claim: They don't want to use out-of-box probability percentage; instead use projected win rate as just a projection/expectation
nodes.why_unusual: Most Salesforce orgs use standard probability fields; explicitly rejecting OOB probability for a custom 'expectation' field is atypical

nodes.text: just like the comment says, like we don't use the out of box probability percentage, but that's more about an expectation. There's another field called projected win rate, which is just that, it's just a projection.
nodes.speaker: Bobby Mehta

nodes.label: They want contact roles PLUS buyer persona criteria, not all contacts on account
nodes.claim: They want contact roles PLUS buyer persona criteria, not all contacts on account
nodes.why_unusual: Standard attribution is either contact-role-only or all-contacts-on-account; wanting a hybrid 'contact roles plus specified personas' is unusual

nodes.text: better solution, I think Harjit suggested this too, is take contact roles, but then in addition to that, say buyer persona, like if it's a benefits leader, and that's the field we would use, there's a field called buyer persona, it would say if that equals benefits leader, HR leader, benefits specialist, then they are also eligible for attribution, but it's only those folks. It's not someone who's like an analytics manager. We don't really care about his engagement.
nodes.speaker: Bobby Mehta

nodes.label: They don't look at pipeline quarterly because health plans renew annually, making quarterly pipeline metrics misleading
nodes.claim: They don't look at pipeline quarterly because health plans renew annually, making quarterly pipeline metrics misleading
nodes.why_unusual: Most B2B SaaS companies obsess over quarterly pipeline; Lyra's annual healthcare enrollment cycle makes quarterly views less relevant

nodes.text: they don't look at pipeline in a quarterly sense because pipeline for them doesn't necessarily close quarterly, right? Because health plans run like very yearly. So it's like towards the end of the year where they're like building a pipeline, building a pipeline. and then there's like the jumps right because some plans they like renew in october or they renew in like this it's like october november december january

nodes.label: Pipeline ROI targets should be 15:1 but booking ROI only 5:1, deliberately setting lower bar for closed revenue to account for sales execution risk
nodes.claim: Pipeline ROI targets should be 15:1 but booking ROI only 5:1, deliberately setting lower bar for closed revenue to account for sales execution risk
nodes.why_unusual: Most orgs set same ratio or higher for bookings; they explicitly expect massive drop-off from pipeline to close and bake that into targets

nodes.text: I set the goal for the team as 15 to 1 and 5 to 1 Ideally, I would love to just set it as five to one and not worry about the 15 to one, but the reality is it could take six months or nine months to get to the five to one, but then the 15 to one, usually stage zero to stage one happens within the first 45 days of an activity.
nodes.speaker: Ryan Shopp

nodes.label: W-model should be abandoned because adding one more touchpoint arbitrarily shifts the midpoint and explodes/changes ROI numbers
nodes.claim: W-model should be abandoned because adding one more touchpoint arbitrarily shifts the midpoint and explodes/changes ROI numbers
nodes.why_unusual: W-model is industry standard; client explicitly rejects it due to instability from touchpoint count changes

nodes.text: I would get rid of W-shape too, because another thing, right, is you have one more touch point, and then your center, your midpoint, is no longer the midpoint. And then that can make it go down. So even if we had the model 100% accurate, that would make numbers go down. If you have a whale, like Western, Southern, or whatever that big one is. it no longer is the midpoint it shifts by one and all of a sudden your ROI numbers explode and change and go down
nodes.speaker: Jacob

nodes.label: Exclude renewals from ROI model but include them in influence model - opposite of typical practice

nodes.label: Overhaul
nodes.before_pain_summary: Highly manual reporting with Steve spending hours building reports in Excel/PowerPoint; no real-time Pipeline visibility; duplicate campaign creation across HubSpot and Salesforce; sales feedback loop broken requiring weekly forcing-function calls; event ROI tracked manually via spreadsheets; no confidence in quarterly forecasting due to moving-target win rate calculations
before_kpis_unhealthy: sales velocity from MQA to SQA
nodes.after_with_revsure: Real-time Pipeline movements and automated weekly reports with color-coded TLDRs saving hours monthly; manual HubSpot campaign ingestion in progress; ABM dashboard with tier filtering launched; still working toward full self-service reporting and deep funnel optimization
after_wins_realized: Automated weekly report template operational
Pipeline movements view replaces quarterly manual builds
Event cost input capability live
336 HubSpot campaigns ingested
ABM tier-based dashboard created
ABM tier dashboard for tier 1/2/3 accounts
Pipeline analytics with push/pull metrics
Incrementality testing for digital paid
Custom opportunity category dimension
Export functionality added across modules
Pipeline predictions dashboard used daily by CRO
Event effectiveness dashboard with ABM tier filtering
Historical pipeline snapshot reports matching Salesforce
Sales Loft integration connected
Custom owner role bucketing for team reporting
after_open_issues: Demandbase API integration blocked (alpha stage)
Facebook sync repeatedly failing
Booking readiness accuracy only ~60% in Q1
Journey visualization inputs need reorganization
Team needs enablement to become self-sufficient
Custom dimensions for verified events/demos needed
Mexico account record type filtering incomplete
Q3 prediction accuracy was severely off
10x pipeline coverage recommendation not credible vs historical 1.5-2x
Unified funnel numbers don't match Demandbase
AI engagement timelines include outdated touches
Addition view stopped working
Limited to 10 product pillars in widgets
Cannot filter to actual sales reps vs all Salesforce users
Large NA bucket in lead source data
Need campaign/channel classification cleanup
Event tracking and spend integration incomplete
Channel classification spreadsheet completion
Salesforce campaign hygiene and naming conventions
Drill-down functionality in dashboard widgets
Team training and adoption beyond CRO
Closed opportunity edit governance
Prior quarter pipeline visibility in current views

nodes.claim: Wants human bias OUT of attribution models, sees 'Wizard of Oz' AI as major industry problem

nodes.text: I don't want to put the weights in because now I'm not in the position to do that. nodes.label: AI agents cannot figure out what isn't working; they only scale what already works

nodes.why_unusual: Contradicts the common vendor pitch that AI will magically fix broken processes; emphasizes human process improvement first

nodes.label: Don't hire agencies or external AI experts; figure it out yourself with your best internal people

nodes.text: there's no like somebody else asked me yesterday like hey i you know i'm rolling out a few platforms some of the some of them are ones that sastry uses do you like recommend anybody to uh any either anybody or like an agency that can help me deploy all this i'm like that's the wrong way to go about it what you got to figure it out yourself

nodes.label: Don't over-invest in data governance before starting AI; 'too much data is too much data'

nodes.text: don't get too hung up on this data governance thing a lot of folks i talk to now especially at bigger orgs um I get very concerned, I'm like, okay, if I add so many agents, I'm going to need governance as a layer, or I'm going to need to clean up all my data and cleaning up all my data or getting all the processes right, it's going to take like two years before I can even think about implementing. I think too much data is too much data.

nodes.label: TRG Screen
nodes.before_pain_summary: No proper marketing attribution; UTM tracking broken since 2021; manual parsing of URLs to extract campaign data; no way to map inbounds until August; duplicated HubSpot forms; sales team not logging leads properly; only new business opportunities tracked, excluding renewals/cross-sell
nodes.after_with_revsure: Building toward unified attribution with campaign channel classification, white space account dashboards with marketing touch correlation, event influence tracking, and cohort-based funnel views
after_wins_realized: Event dashboard with last-touch and influence attribution
White space priority account filtering
Post-opportunity creation influence tracking
Ability to filter by customer health score and sentiment
Cohort funnel views available
Terrence dashboard created with 6 core views
Event campaign attribution showing prospect/customer/lost client breakdown
Lead prioritization by account tier and activity recency
Last touch campaign write-back to Salesforce enabled
after_open_issues: Campaign channel classification still in progress
Daily sync lag vs real-time
Custom fields require manual backend ingestion
Sales team adoption for lead logging
Need to add product count/penetration fields
Gong integration not yet built
Campaign member status not yet synced (registered vs attended)
Custom year one ARR field prevents tracking amount changes
Redwood account still has adoption challenges
Some data sync delays causing temporary metric inaccuracies

nodes.label: Adding a Stage Zero that is explicitly excluded from all pipeline metrics - a pre-pipeline holding stage
nodes.claim: Adding a Stage Zero that is explicitly excluded from all pipeline metrics - a pre-pipeline holding stage
nodes.why_unusual: Most RevOps teams fight to include MORE in pipeline, not create explicit non-pipeline stages. Stage Zero with 0% probability and omitted from forecast is counter to typical 'pipeline coverage' mentality.

nodes.text: we're essentially going to add a new stage, but that stage is going to be stage zero. And this will be a stage that will be at the beginning of the cycle omitted from any pipeline or anything like that. So it won't be included as It won't be included as part of any pipeline or anything.
nodes.speaker: Cory Perry

nodes.label: Using custom 'year one ARR' field instead of standard Amount field because Amount includes one-time fees
nodes.claim: Using custom 'year one ARR' field instead of standard Amount field because Amount includes one-time fees
nodes.why_unusual: Most companies just use standard Amount field; deliberately excluding implementation/professional services revenue from pipeline metrics is a sophisticated (and unusual) approach to ARR purity.

nodes.text: it's a field called year one ARR. And so that's kind of our, that's kind of our like revenue field, I guess, if you want to say that's like our recurring revenue field is the year one ARR, whereas the amount field typically can be a combination of both whatever the recurring revenue is, and then also if there are any like one-time fees or implementation fees or anything like that they all get rolled up into that amount like that standard amount field
nodes.speaker: Cory Perry

nodes.label: Terrence (CRO) cares more about product breakdown than source/channel breakdown of pipeline
nodes.claim: Terrence (CRO) cares more about product breakdown than source/channel breakdown of pipeline
nodes.why_unusual: CROs typically obsess over 'where is pipeline coming from' (marketing vs sales); asking 'what products is it' first is unusual product-centric revenue leadership.

nodes.text: Terrence is typically asking about like cool. What products is that? Like what is the product breakdown or product makeup of that? versus you know, like Where it's coming from and you know things like that.
